content:
5 min read
1 hour ago

--

The first time a WebSocket feature broke in production, the logs said everything was fine. The server thought the client was connected. The client thought it was connected. And yet messages just stopped arriving. No error, no disconnect event, nothing. Just silence.
That bug taught me the most important thing about WebSockets. A connection that looks alive is not the same as a connection that works.
If you have only ever used plain HTTP, the mental model feels simple. You open a socket, you send messages both ways, you close it. In practice the open socket is the easy part. Everything after it is where the pain lives. Here are the problems that actually bit me, and the fixes that stuck.
The connection dies quietly
The classic failure is the half open connection. A laptop goes to sleep, a phone switches from wifi to cellular, a load balancer times out an idle socket. On one side the connection is gone. On the other side nothing fires. Your onclose handler never runs because the network never got the chance to say goodbye.
You cannot trust the socket to tell you it is dead. You have to ask.
The fix is a heartbeat. The client sends a small ping on a timer, and the server replies with a pong. If the client sends three pings and hears nothing back, it treats the connection as dead and tears it down itself. Same logic on the server. If a client has not pinged in a while, drop it and free the resources.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Pick the interval based on how fast you need to notice a drop, not on some default you copied. Fifteen seconds was a good middle ground for me. Too short and you waste battery on mobile. Too long and a dead connection lingers.
Reconnecting is harder than connecting
So the heartbeat catches the dead socket. Now what? You reconnect. Sounds easy. It is not.
The naive version reconnects instantly in a tight loop. The moment your server hiccups, every client that dropped tries to reconnect at the same second. That stampede can keep a recovering server on its knees. I have done this. It is not fun to watch.
What works is backoff with jitter. Wait a little longer after each failed attempt, and add a random delay so clients do not all retry in lockstep.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cap the maximum delay so a client that has been offline for an hour still tries roughly every thirty seconds once it wakes up. And reset the attempt counter once a connection survives for a few seconds, otherwise a flaky network leaves you stuck at the longest delay forever.
Messages do not always survive a reconnect
Here is the part that surprised me most. Reconnecting gives you a new socket, but it does not give you back the messages you missed while you were gone.
If a user closes their laptop for two minutes, comes back, and your app silently picks up only the new messages, they will see a hole in their data and never know it. The screen looks fine. It is just wrong.
Two patterns saved me here. First, every client tracks the last message id it actually processed. On reconnect, it tells the server “I last saw 4812, send me anything after that.” The server replays the gap. Second, make your message handlers safe to run twice. Networks will deliver the same message again, and a handler that assumes it only runs once will double count, double charge, or double post.
Idempotency is boring to build and it saves you at three in the morning.
You will send too much, too fast
WebSockets make it trivial to push every tiny update the instant it happens. That is the trap. A live cursor or a fast moving price feed can fire hundreds of updates a second, and the browser cannot paint that fast anyway. The tab heats up, the fan spins, and the user gets a worse experience than if you had sent less.
Batch on the server. Collect updates for a short window, fifty to a hundred milliseconds, and send one combined frame. The user cannot perceive the difference, and your client stops drowning. On the receiving side, hold the newest state and render on the next animation frame instead of on every message.
Less data, sent smarter, almost always beats more data sent instantly.
Scaling breaks the simple mental model
One server holding all the sockets is easy. The trouble starts at the second server.
A WebSocket is a long lived connection pinned to one machine. If user A is connected to server one and user B is connected to server two, and A sends a message meant for B, server one has no idea where B is. Your in memory list of connected clients only knows about its own.
The standard fix is a shared message bus, something like Redis pub sub, sitting behind your servers. Each server subscribes to the channels its connected clients care about. When a message comes in, it gets published to the bus, and whichever server holds the target client picks it up and delivers it. Your application code stops caring which machine anyone landed on.
Also remember sticky sessions at the load balancer. A WebSocket cannot bounce between servers mid connection, so the balancer has to keep each client pinned to the same backend for the life of the socket.
A few smaller things that bit me
Authentication is awkward because the browser WebSocket API will not let you set custom headers on the handshake. I ended up passing a short lived token as a query parameter and validating it on connect, then refreshing over the open socket. Do not put a long lived secret in the URL. URLs end up in logs.
Watch your message size. Some proxies and servers quietly cap frame size, and a large payload just vanishes with a confusing close code. Send a reference and let the client fetch the heavy data over HTTP.
And test on a real flaky network, not just localhost. On localhost the socket never drops, latency is zero, and every bug I described stays hidden until your users find it for you.
The takeaway
WebSockets are not hard because the protocol is complicated. They are hard because they hand you a stateful, long lived connection over an unreliable network, and they keep insisting everything is fine when it is not.
So assume the connection will lie. Add a heartbeat so you find out fast. Reconnect with backoff and jitter so you do not pile on. Track message ids so nobody loses data, and make handlers idempotent so nobody gets it twice. Do those four things up front and most of the late night pages go away.
What is the strangest WebSocket bug you have shipped? I would love to hear it.
